paper.title_en: A supermassive black hole binary in $^{3}$C 454.3: Orbital dynamics and nanohertz gravitational-wave emission
paper.abstract_en: The blazar 3C 454.3 is considered a promising candidate for hosting a supermassive black hole binary (SMBHB) capable of generating gravitational waves in the nanohertz frequency range. Using long-term multifrequency radio observations spanning nearly six decades, we identify a persistent quasi-periodic modulation with a characteristic timescale of 2.3 yr in the observer’s frame. Interpreting this variability within a relativistic jet framework and adopting a Lorentz factor of γ = 5 yields an intrinsic orbital period of approximately 31 yr. The derived binary parameters correspond to component masses of M ≈ 2.4 × 1010 M⊙ and m ≈ 6.8 × 109 M⊙, separated by a Keplerian distance of a ≈ 4.6 × 1017 cm. Under these conditions, the system is expected to radiate gravitational waves at an observed frequency of fGW ≈ 1.1 × 10−9 Hz (≈ 2.0 × 10−9 Hz in the source frame), placing it firmly within the sensitivity window of pulsar timing arrays (PTAs). The estimated characteristic strain amplitude is h0 ≈ (7–9) × 10−16.The binary is predicted to merge on a timescale of approximately 5.7 × 104 yr. Although gravitational-wave emission drives a secular orbital evolution, the expected period change is extremely small, corresponding to a cumulative shift of only a few days over the full duration of available observations. Such a signal is far below the level that can be extracted from the intrinsically variable, Doppler-boosted electromagnetic emission of the source. These results support the interpretation of 3C 454.3 as a potential nanohertz gravitational-wave emitter and suggest that exceptionally massive SMBHB systems of this type may contribute not only to future PTA continuous-wave detections but also to the large-scale anisotropy of the stochastic gravitational-wave background.
